era_context: Early Bronze Age III in the Levant and Mesopotamia. The Old Kingdom of Egypt is in decline (late Dynasty 6, c. 2345–2181 BCE); the First Intermediate Period is beginning. In Mesopotamia, the Akkadian Empire under Naram-Sin (r. 2254–2218 BCE) is at or near its territorial zenith. The Indus Valley Civilisation is in its mature phase (c. 2600–1900 BCE). In China, the Xia Dynasty is traditionally dated to this period, though archaeological confirmation remains contested.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Americas (no written records; archaeological record sparse for this period)

events.title: Akkadian Empire under Naram-Sin at territorial maximum
events.description: Naram-Sin of Akkad (r. 2254–2218 BCE) consolidates Akkadian control over Mesopotamia and extends influence into the Levant, Anatolia, and possibly the Zagros Mountains. He declares himself a god and establishes a new model of divine kingship. His reign represents the apex of Akkadian imperial power before internal instability and external pressure from the Gutians begin to erode central authority.

sources.name: Akkadian royal inscriptions and victory stelae (e.g., Naram-Sin Stele from Susa)

events.certainty_note: Naram-Sin's reign is well-attested in contemporary inscriptions and administrative records. Absolute dating to 2254–2218 BCE is based on Mesopotamian chronology, which has ±50–100 year uncertainty at this depth. The year -2519 falls within his reign with high probability.

events.title: Indus Valley Civilisation in mature phase
events.description: The Indus Valley Civilisation (c. 2600–1900 BCE) is at its height, with major urban centres at Harappa, Mohenjo-daro, and Dholavira. Extensive trade networks connect the Indus to Mesopotamia, the Persian Gulf, and Central Asia. The civilisation is characterised by standardised weights and measures, planned urban layouts, and an undeciphered script. No monumental temples or palaces have been identified, suggesting a non-hierarchical or differently-organised political structure.

sources.name: Archaeological excavations at Harappa, Mohenjo-daro, Dholavira, and other sites

events.certainty_note: The mature phase of the Indus Valley Civilisation is well-established archaeologically. Absolute dating relies on radiocarbon and stratigraphic correlation; -2519 falls within the accepted range for the mature Harappan period.

events.title: Egyptian Old Kingdom in late decline
events.description: The Old Kingdom of Egypt (Dynasty 6, c. 2345–2181 BCE) is in its final phase. Royal authority is fragmenting; provincial governors (nomarchs) accumulate power and wealth. The reign of Pepi II (traditionally dated c. 2278–2184 BCE) is marked by administrative weakness and the rise of local autonomy. The First Intermediate Period (c. 2181–2055 BCE) is imminent. Monumental building projects decline; the state's ability to mobilise labour and resources is visibly diminishing.

sources.name: Egyptian administrative papyri and tomb inscriptions

events.certainty_note: The decline of the Old Kingdom is well-documented in administrative records and tomb inscriptions. Absolute dating of Pepi II's reign is debated; estimates range from c. 2278–2184 BCE to c. 2250–2150 BCE. The year -2519 falls within the probable range of his reign.

disconfirming_evidence: The traditional dating of the Xia Dynasty to this period (often cited as c. 2070–1600 BCE) lacks contemporary written or archaeological confirmation. Modern scholarship increasingly views the Xia as legendary or semi-legendary; no contemporary inscriptions or securely dated archaeological layer has been definitively attributed to a 'Xia' polity. The Shang Dynasty (c. 1600–1046 BCE) is the earliest Chinese dynasty with confirmed contemporary written records (oracle bone script). Inclusion of the Xia in this period is based on traditional chronology, not archaeological evidence.
historiographic_note: Documentation for -2519 is heavily skewed toward Mesopotamia and Egypt, where written records and monumental archaeology are abundant. The Indus Valley Civilisation is known primarily through archaeology; its political structure, language, and historical narratives remain opaque. South Asian, Central Asian, and East Asian history for this period relies heavily on later compilations (Sumerian King List, Manetho, Sima Qian) or oral tradition. Sub-Saharan Africa, the Americas, and Northern Europe are essentially undocumented for this year. Mesopotamian chronology itself carries ±50–100 year uncertainty at this depth, making precise year-by-year attribution problematic.

graph_edges.note: Naram-Sin's imperial overreach and internal instability contributed to the Akkadian Empire's collapse within a generation.
The fragmentation of Old Kingdom authority in this period directly precipitated the First Intermediate Period.
